title: Nelson, Pitt, Fox
content:
آ آ آ آ To mute and to material things
آ آ آ آ New life revolving summer brings;
آ آ آ آ The genial call dead Nature hears,
آ آ آ آ And in her glory reappears.
آ آ آ آ But oh, my Countryï؟½s wintry state
آ آ آ آ What second spring shall renovate?
آ آ آ آ What powerful call shall bid arise
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ The buried warlike and the wise;

آ آ آ آ The mind that thought for Britainï؟½s weal,
آ آ آ آ The hand that graspï؟½d the victor steel?
آ آ آ آ The vernal sun new life bestows
آ آ آ آ Even on the meanest flower that blows;
آ آ آ آ But vainly, vainly may he shine
آ آ آ آ Where glory weeps oï؟½er Nelsonï؟½s shrine;
آ آ آ آ And vainly pierce the solemn gloom
آ آ آ آ That shrouds, O Pitt, thy hallowï؟½d tomb!

آ آ آ آ Deep graved in every British heart,
آ آ آ آ O never let those names depart!
آ آ آ آ Say to your sons, Lo, here his grave,
آ آ آ آ Who victor died on Gadite wave!
آ آ آ آ To him, as to the burning levin,
آ آ آ آ Short, bright, resistless course was given.
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½er his countryï؟½s foes were found
آ آ آ آ Was heard the fated thunderï؟½s sound,
آ آ آ آ Till burst the bolt on yonder shore,
آ آ آ آ Rollï؟½d, blazed, destroyï؟½d and was no more.

آ آ آ آ Nor mourn ye less his perishï؟½d worth,
آ آ آ آ Who bade the conqueror go forth,
آ آ آ آ And launchï؟½d that thunderbolt of war
آ آ آ آ On Egypt, Hafnia, Trafalgar;
آ آ آ آ Who, born to guide such high emprise,
آ آ آ آ For Britainï؟½s weal was early wise;
آ آ آ آ Alas! to whom the Almighty gave,
آ آ آ آ For Britainï؟½s sins, an early grave!
آ آ آ آ His worth, who in his mightiest hour
آ آ آ آ A bauble held the pride of power,
آ آ آ آ Spurnï؟½d at the sordid lust of pelf,
آ آ آ آ And served his Albion for herself;
آ آ آ آ Who, when the frantic crowd amain
آ آ آ آ Strainï؟½d at subjectionï؟½s bursting rein,
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er their wild mood full conquest gainï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ The pride he would not crush, restrainï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Showï؟½d their fierce zeal a worthier cause,
آ آ آ آ And brought the freemanï؟½s arm to aid the freemanï؟½s laws.

آ آ آ آ Hadst thou but lived, though strippï؟½d of power,
آ آ آ آ A watchman on the lonely tower,
آ آ آ آ Thy thrilling trump had roused the land,
آ آ آ آ When fraud or danger were at hand;
آ آ آ آ By thee, as by the beacon-light,
آ آ آ آ Our pilots had kept course aright;
آ آ آ آ As some proud column, though alone,
آ آ آ آ Thy strength had proppï؟½d the tottering throne.
آ آ آ آ Now is the stately column broke,
آ آ آ آ The beacon-light is quenchï؟½d in smoke,
آ آ آ آ The trumpetï؟½s silver voice is still,
آ آ آ آ The warder silent on the hill!

آ آ آ آ O think, how to his latest day,
آ آ آ آ When Death, just hovering, claimï؟½d his prey,
آ آ آ آ With Palinureï؟½s unalterï؟½d mood
آ آ آ آ Firm at his dangerous post he stood;
آ آ آ آ Each call for needful rest repellï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ With dying hand the rudder held,
آ آ آ آ Till in his fall with fateful sway
آ آ آ آ The steerage of the realm gave way.
آ آ آ آ Then, while on Britainï؟½s thousand plains
آ آ آ آ One polluted church remains,
آ آ آ آ Whose peaceful bells neï؟½er sent around
آ آ آ آ The bloody tocsinï؟½s maddening sound,
آ آ آ آ But still upon the hallowï؟½d day
آ آ آ آ Convoke the swains to praise and pray;
آ آ آ آ While faith and civil peace are dear,
آ آ آ آ Grace this cold marble with a tear:
آ آ آ آ He who preserved them, Pitt, lies here!

آ آ آ آ Nor yet suppress the generous sigh,
آ آ آ آ Because his rival slumbers nigh;
آ آ آ آ Nor be thy Requiescat dumb
آ آ آ آ Lest it be said oï؟½er Foxï؟½s tomb.
آ آ آ آ For talents mourn, untimely lost,
آ آ آ آ When best employï؟½d, and wanted most;
آ آ آ آ Mourn genius high, and lore profound,
آ آ آ آ And wit that loved to play, not wound;
آ آ آ آ And all the reasoning powers divine
آ آ آ آ To penetrate, resolve, combine;
آ آ آ آ And feelings keen, and fancyï؟½s glow,
آ آ آ آ They sleep with him who sleeps below:
آ آ آ آ And, if thou mournï؟½st they could not save
آ آ آ آ From error him who owns this grave,
آ آ آ آ Be every harsher thought suppressï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And sacred be the last long rest.
آ آ آ آ Here, where the end of earthly things
آ آ آ آ Lays heroes, patriots, bards, and kings;
آ آ آ آ Where stiff the hand, and still the tongue,
آ آ آ آ Of those who fought, and spoke, and sung;
آ آ آ آ Here, where the fretted vaults prolong
آ آ آ آ The distant notes of holy song,
آ آ آ آ As if some angel spoke agen,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½All peace on earth, good-will to menï؟½;
آ آ آ آ If ever from an English heart,
آ آ آ آ O, here let prejudice depart,
آ آ آ آ And, partial feeling cast aside,
آ آ آ آ Record that Fox a Briton died!
آ آ آ آ When Europe crouchï؟½d to Franceï؟½s yoke,
آ آ آ آ And Austria bent, and Prussia broke,
آ آ آ آ And the firm Russianï؟½s purpose brave
آ آ آ آ Was barterï؟½d by a timorous slave,
آ آ آ آ Even then dishonourï؟½s peace he spurnï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ The sullied olive-branch returnï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Stood for his countryï؟½s glory fast,
آ آ آ آ And nailï؟½d her colours to the mast!
آ آ آ آ Heaven, to reward his firmness, gave
آ آ آ آ A portion in this honourï؟½d grave;
آ آ آ آ And neï؟½er held marble in its trust
آ آ آ آ Of two such wondrous men the dust.

آ آ آ آ With more than mortal powers endowï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ How high they soarï؟½d above the crowd!
آ آ آ آ Theirs was no common party race,
آ آ آ آ Jostling by dark intrigue for place;
آ آ آ آ Like fabled gods, their mighty war
آ آ آ آ Shook realms and nations in its jar;
آ آ آ آ Beneath each banner proud to stand,
آ آ آ آ Lookï؟½d up the noblest of the land,
آ آ آ آ Till through the British world were known
آ آ آ آ The names of Pitt and Fox alone.
آ آ آ آ Spells of such force no wizard grave
آ آ آ آ Eï؟½er framed in dark Thessalian cave,
آ آ آ آ Though his could drain the ocean dry,
آ آ آ آ And force the planets from the sky.
آ آ آ آ These spells are spent, and, spent with these,
آ آ آ آ The wine of life is on the lees.
آ آ آ آ Genius, and taste, and talent gone,
آ آ آ آ For ever tombï؟½d beneath the stone,
آ آ آ آ Where, taming thought to human pride!
آ آ آ آ The mighty chiefs sleep side by side.
آ آ آ آ Drop upon Foxï؟½s grave the tear,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twill trickle to his rivalï؟½s bier;
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er Pittï؟½s the mournful requiem sound,
آ آ آ آ And Foxï؟½s shall the notes rebound.
آ آ آ آ The solemn echo seems to cry,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Here let their discord with them die.
آ آ آ آ Speak not for those a separate doom
آ آ آ آ Whom fate made Brothers in the tomb;
آ آ آ آ But search the land of living men,
آ آ آ آ Where wilt thou find their like agen?ï؟½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Walter Scott (Sir) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Walter Scott (Sir)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
